lesson.title: Statutory and Non-statutory Regulations
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To enable learners to correctly identify and distinguish between statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance within the UK electrical industry.
anchorFacts: To enable learners to correctly identify and distinguish between statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance within the UK electrical industry.
Why this matters: Understanding the 'why' prevents learners from viewing regulations as arbitrary paperwork.
taskConstraints: Introduce the lesson naturally, but do not start the first teaching chunk yet.

steps: The Purpose of Industry Regulations
Explanation B
Statutory Regulations
Non-statutory Regulations/guidance

steps.title: The Purpose of Industry Regulations
steps.objective: Explain why the electrical industry is governed by specific regulations and guidance.
anchorFacts: In the electrical industry, regulations exist to ensure safety, consistency, and legal accountability. Electricity is inherently dangerous; it can cause fires, serious injury, or death if not handled correctly. Because of this, we cannot leave installation methods to personal preference.

Regulations provide a common set of rules so that every electrician in the UK works to the same high standard. This consistency means that any qualified person can walk onto a site and understand how the system was built. These rules are not just 'paperwork' or 'red tape'; they are the minimum requirements necessary to protect the public and the workforce. By following these standards, we ensure that installations are reliable and, most importantly, safe for continued use.
misconceptionsToWatch: Regulations are only for large industrial sites.
Think about why we need everyone to follow the same safety rules instead of making them up as they go.
taskConstraints: The role of standards in ensuring safety, consistency, and legal compliance in electrical work.
Understanding the 'why' prevents learners from viewing regulations as arbitrary paperwork.
steps.questionIntent: The role of standards in ensuring safety, consistency, and legal compliance in electrical work.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary reason for having industry regulations in electrical work?
What inherent risk of electricity makes these regulations necessary?
What does consistency allow a qualified person to do when walking onto a new site?
answerGuidance: Understand how the system was built

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way having a common set of rules across the UK improve safety for an electrician taking over a job from someone else?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Understanding the 'why' prevents learners from viewing regulations as arbitrary paperwork.
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures the previous worker used the same standards; Removes guesswork regarding installation methods; Allows any qualified person to understand the system layout; Prevents dangerous variations based on personal preference
steps.hint: Think about why we need everyone to follow the same safety rules instead of making them up as they go.

steps.title: The Two Categories of Rules
steps.objective: Differentiate between Statutory and Non-statutory regulations.
anchorFacts: All rules in our industry fall into two main categories: Statutory and Non-statutory. It is vital to understand the difference between a 'law' and 'guidance'.

Statutory means it is the law of the land, passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to fines or imprisonment. Non-statutory regulations are expert guidance and codes of practice. These are the 'how-to' manuals written by industry bodies. While a non-statutory document itself is not a law, it is often used in court to prove whether or not a person was working competently. If you follow the non-statutory guidance, you are usually meeting your statutory legal duties.
misconceptionsToWatch: One is a law passed by Parliament, the other is expert guidance on how to do the job.
steps.questionIntent: Explanation B

basicQuestions.questionText: What term describes a rule that is the law of the land passed by Parliament?
What type of offense is committed if a statutory regulation is broken?
What category do expert guidance and codes of practice fall into?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it important for an electrician to follow non-statutory guidance even if it is not a law passed by Parliament?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explanation B
deeperAnswerGuidance: Used in court to prove competence; Demonstrates the worker met statutory legal duties; Provides the technical method to comply with the law; Acts as the industry benchmark for safety
steps.hint: One is a law passed by Parliament, the other is expert guidance on how to do the job.

steps.title: Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
anchorFacts: Statutory regulations are mandatory requirements that must be followed. The primary law is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This acts as an 'Umbrella Act,' setting out general safety duties for everyone at work.

Underneath this umbrella, we have more specific laws. The Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is the most important statutory document for us, as it focuses specifically on electrical hazards. Other examples include COSHH, which stands for Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (covering things like solder fumes or dust), and PPE, which stands for Personal Protective Equipment. PPE regulations require you to use safety gear like hard hats and boots. Because these are statutory, failure to comply is a legal breach, not just a technical mistake.
misconceptionsToWatch: Statutory regulations like EAWR and HASAWA are mandatory laws.
steps.questionIntent: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the full name of the 'Umbrella Act' for workplace safety?
answerGuidance: Health and Safety at Work Act

basicQuestions.questionText: Which specific statutory regulation focuses on electrical hazards in the workplace?
What does the acronym COSHH stand for?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the relationship between HASAWA and EAWR ensure that electrical risks are managed legally?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
deeperAnswerGuidance: HASAWA provides the general safety framework; EAWR provides the specific legal requirements for electricity; Both are statutory and mandatory to follow; EAWR sits under the HASAWA umbrella to target specific hazards
steps.hint: Statutory regulations like EAWR and HASAWA are mandatory laws.

steps.title: Non-statutory Regulations and Guidance
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
anchorFacts: Non-statutory regulations are the professional's guidebooks. The most famous example is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' Although BS 7671 is not a law passed by Parliament, it is the national standard that tells us exactly how to design and install electrical systems safely.

Other non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified 'Pocket Guide' for common tasks, and various Guidance Notes. These documents are essential because they provide the technical detail that the law (like EAWR) does not. For example, the law says an installation must be safe; BS 7671 tells you exactly what size cable to use to make it safe. Following these ensures you are working to the current industry benchmark.
misconceptionsToWatch: BS 7671 is the main industry standard, but it is technically non-statutory guidance.
steps.questionIntent: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the common name for the BS 7671 national standard?
Which non-statutory document acts as a simplified pocket guide for common tasks?
What do non-statutory documents provide that the law usually lacks?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If the law (EAWR) says an installation must be safe, why is BS 7671 necessary for a working electrician?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
deeperAnswerGuidance: The law states the requirement but not the method
BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions
It defines exact requirements like cable sizes
It ensures the installation meets the industry benchmark for safety
steps.hint: BS 7671 is the main industry standard, but it is technically non-statutory guidance.

steps.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Acts
steps.objective: Identify and map common statutory regulations used in the industry.
anchorFacts: To work legally, you must recognize the specific names of statutory regulations. HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) is the foundation for all workplace safety. EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) specifically targets electrical safety in the workplace.

Other key laws include PUWER, which stands for Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations; this ensures your tools and machinery are safe to use. ESQCR stands for Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, which mainly concerns the companies that supply and distribute power. CDM stands for Construction (Design and Management) Regulations, which manage safety during building projects. Each of these is a statutory requirement, meaning they are legally binding. You must use these specific titles and acronyms correctly when identifying the laws that govern your work.
misconceptionsToWatch: Look for acronyms like HASAWA, EAWR, and PUWER; these are all statutory laws.
steps.questionIntent: Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation ensures that tools and machinery are safe to use?
answerGuidance: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the acronym ESQCR stand for?
answerGuidance: Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: Which regulations manage safety specifically during building and construction projects?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason an electrician be aware of PUWER in addition to EAWR when performing a task?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.
deeperAnswerGuidance: EAWR covers the electrical safety of the system; PUWER covers the safety of the tools being used; Both are separate statutory requirements that must be met; Ensures both the work activity and the equipment are legal
steps.hint: Look for acronyms like HASAWA, EAWR, and PUWER; these are all statutory laws.

steps.title: Worked Example: Classifying Site Documents
steps.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are preparing to install a new sub-main in a commercial unit. On your desk, you have three documents: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). You need to categorize these correctly before starting the Risk Assessment.

Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for the 'Acts' or 'Regulations' passed by Parliament. HASAWA is the 'Umbrella Act' and is statutory. The EAWR specifically covers electrical safety and is also statutory. These are the laws of the land; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.

Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for the technical standards and codes of practice. BS 7671 (The Big Blue Book) is the national standard for electrical installations. While it is the industry benchmark, it is non-statutory guidance. It tells you 'how' to install the cable to meet the 'legal' requirements set out in the EAWR.
Step 3: Apply the priority. You recognize that while you use BS 7671 to design the circuit, you are doing so to ensure you do not breach the statutory requirements of the EAWR.
Final Result: HASAWA and EAWR are classified as Statutory Regulations (The Law). BS 7671 is classified as Non-statutory Guidance (The Method).

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are preparing to install a new sub-main in a commercial unit. On your desk, you have three documents: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). You need to categorize these correctly before starting the Risk Assessment.
steps: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for the 'Acts' or 'Regulations' passed by Parliament. HASAWA is the 'Umbrella Act' and is statutory. The EAWR specifically covers electrical safety and is also statutory. These are the laws of the land; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.

Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for the technical standards and codes of practice. BS 7671 (The Big Blue Book) is the national standard for electrical installations. While it is the industry benchmark, it is non-statutory guidance. It tells you 'how' to install the cable to meet the 'legal' requirements set out in the EAWR.
Apply the priority. You recognize that while you use BS 7671 to design the circuit, you are doing so to ensure you do not breach the statutory requirements of the EAWR.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Statutory regulations are mandatory laws you must obey to avoid prosecution; non-statutory guidance provides the technical instructions to ensure your work is safe enough to meet those laws.

steps.hint: Always ask: 'Is this a law passed by Parliament, or is it a technical manual on how to do the job?'

steps.title: Guided Practice: Categorising Site Safety and Technical Documents
steps.objective: To correctly identify statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance in a site-based scenario.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are working on a new build. You are wearing safety boots (PPE Regulations), you have just read the safety data sheet for a new adhesive (COSHH), and you are checking a cable size in your On-Site Guide.
Step 1: Look for the 'Statutory' items. These are the laws of the land. PPE Regulations and COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) are both legal requirements passed by Parliament.
Step 2: Look for the 'Non-statutory' items. These are the technical guides. The On-Site Guide is a handbook that provides guidance on how to meet the standards, but it is not a law itself.
Step 3: Group them clearly into the two categories.
steps.questionIntent: To correctly identify statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance in a site-based scenario.

taskSkeleton.scenario: Scenario: You are working on a new build. You are wearing safety boots (PPE Regulations), you have just read the safety data sheet for a new adhesive (COSHH), and you are checking a cable size in your On-Site Guide.
steps: Step 1: Look for the 'Statutory' items. These are the laws of the land. PPE Regulations and COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) are both legal requirements passed by Parliament.
Step 2: Look for the 'Non-statutory' items. These are the technical guides. The On-Site Guide is a handbook that provides guidance on how to meet the standards, but it is not a law itself.
Step 3: Group them clearly into the two categories.
requiredOutputs: PPE Regulations and COSHH. Non-statutory Guidance: On-Site Guide.

steps.questionText: Based on the scenario, identify which items are Statutory Regulations and which item is Non-statutory Guidance.
answerGuidance: PPE Regulations and COSHH. Non-statutory Guidance: On-Site Guide.
steps.hint: Remember: Regulations and Acts are usually statutory (the law), while guides and handbooks are non-statutory.

steps.title: Practice: Distinguishing Legal Status
steps.objective: To independently classify industry documents and explain their legal distinction.
anchorFacts: A trainee asks you why they need to look at both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671).
You need to explain the status of each document to help them understand which one is the law and which one is the technical method used to comply with that law.
steps.questionIntent: To independently classify industry documents and explain their legal distinction.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A trainee asks you why they need to look at both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671).
steps: You need to explain the status of each document to help them understand which one is the law and which one is the technical method used to comply with that law.
requiredOutputs: EAWR is Statutory and BS 7671 is Non-statutory. The primary difference is that statutory regulations are mandatory laws where failure to comply is a criminal offence, whereas non-statutory guidance provides the professional standards to meet those laws.

steps.questionText: From the scenario, identify the specific technical answer that best fits.
answerGuidance: EAWR is Statutory and BS 7671 is Non-statutory. The primary difference is that statutory regulations are mandatory laws where failure to comply is a criminal offence, whereas non-statutory guidance provides the professional standards to meet those laws.
steps.hint: Think about which one is 'The Electrician's Law' and which one is 'The Big Blue Book' of technical rules.

steps.title: Integrative Close: The Relationship Between Law and Guidance
steps.objective: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a professional electrical context.
anchorFacts: In the electrical industry, we don't just pick one set of rules to follow. We use statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance together to ensure our work is both legal and technically sound.

Statutory regulations like the EAWR set the mandatory legal requirements, while non-statutory documents like BS 7671 provide the technical methods we use to meet those legal obligations. Understanding how these two categories interact is the mark of a professional electrician.
steps.questionIntent: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a professional electrical context.

taskSkeleton.scenario: In the electrical industry, we don't just pick one set of rules to follow. We use statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance together to ensure our work is both legal and technically sound.
steps: Statutory regulations like the EAWR set the mandatory legal requirements, while non-statutory documents like BS 7671 provide the technical methods we use to meet those legal obligations. Understanding how these two categories interact is the mark of a professional electrician.
requiredOutputs: The response should identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation and BS 7671 is non-statutory guidance . It must explain that they work together because the EAWR sets the legal safety requirements, while BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions and methods needed to achieve that legal level of safety.

steps.questionText: Imagine you are explaining to a new apprentice why they need to use both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671). Explain the different legal status of these two documents and how they work together to ensure a safe, legal installation.
answerGuidance: The response should identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation and BS 7671 is non-statutory guidance. It must explain that they work together because the EAWR sets the legal safety requirements, while BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions and methods needed to achieve that legal level of safety.
steps.hint: Think about which one is the 'Law of the Land' and which one is the 'Professional's Guidebook' that tells you how to meet that law.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To enable learners to correctly identify and distinguish between statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance within the UK electrical industry.
anchorFacts: The electrical industry is governed by a strict hierarchy of rules designed to prevent injury and death.
Learners must distinguish between 'Statutory' (the law) and 'Non-statutory' (technical guidance).
Key documents include HASAWA, EAWR, and BS 7671.

steps.title: The Purpose of Industry Regulations
steps.objective: Explain why the electrical industry is governed by specific regulations and guidance.
anchorFacts: Electricity is inherently dangerous because it is invisible and odorless; regulations provide the only reliable safeguard against risks like electric shock, arcing, and thermal burns.
Regulations remove 'individual interpretation' of safety, ensuring every electrician works to the same high standard across the UK.
Every person on a site has a 'Duty of Care' to themselves, their colleagues, and the public to ensure the installation is safe for continued use.
Consistency allows any qualified person to walk onto a site and immediately understand the safety and design of the system.
misconceptionsToWatch: Regulations are just 'red tape' or paperwork.
Safety is a matter of personal opinion rather than a standardized requirement.
taskConstraints: Must mention that electricity cannot be seen or smelled.
Must define 'Duty of Care'.
steps.questionIntent: The role of standards in ensuring safety, consistency, and legal compliance in electrical work.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Understanding the 'why' prevents learners from viewing regulations as arbitrary paperwork.

basicQuestions.questionText: Why is electricity considered particularly dangerous compared to other site hazards?
What legal concept describes the responsibility an electrician has toward the safety of others?
What do regulations remove to ensure that all electricians work to the same safety standard?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way removing 'individual interpretation' through regulations specifically improve safety when a new electrician takes over a partially finished job?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures the previous worker used the same standards; Removes guesswork regarding installation methods; Allows any qualified person to understand the system layout; Prevents dangerous variations based on personal preference
steps.hint: Think about how a common set of rules prevents different people from doing the same job in different, potentially dangerous ways.

steps.title: The Two Categories of Rules
steps.objective: Differentiate between Statutory and Non-statutory regulations.
anchorFacts: Statutory regulations are 'Criminal Law' passed by Parliament. Breaking them is a criminal offense that can lead to prosecution, heavy fines, or imprisonment.
Non-statutory regulations are expert guidance and codes of practice. These are often 'Civil' or 'Contractual' rather than direct law.

While non-statutory documents like BS 7671 are not laws themselves, they are 'deemed to satisfy' the law. This means if you follow them, you have a strong legal defense that you have met your statutory duties.
Statutory = The Law (Mandatory). Non-statutory = The Method (Guidance to meet the law).
misconceptionsToWatch: Non-statutory means 'optional' and can be ignored without consequence.
BS 7671 is a law passed by Parliament.
taskConstraints: Must distinguish between Criminal Law and Civil/Contractual guidance.
Must use the phrase 'deemed to satisfy'.
steps.questionIntent: Differentiate between the legal status of statutory and non-statutory documents.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the relationship between guidance and legal defense.

basicQuestions.questionText: What legal category do statutory regulations fall into?
What phrase describes how following non-statutory guidance proves you have met your legal duties in court?
What is the primary legal consequence of breaching a statutory regulation?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If BS 7671 is non-statutory, why would an electrician still be in legal trouble if they ignored it and an accident occurred?
deeperAnswerGuidance: They would fail to prove they met their statutory duty of care; It is used in court to prove competence; Ignoring industry standards suggests negligence; It is the benchmark for safe working
steps.hint: Think about how a judge would view someone who ignored the industry's official safety manual.

steps.title: Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
anchorFacts: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA) is the 'Umbrella Act' for all workplace safety. Section 2 outlines the duties of employers, while Section 7 outlines the duties of employees to look after themselves and others.

The Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is the most critical statutory document for electricians. It contains 33 regulations covering everything from the design of systems to working near live conductors.
Other statutory examples include COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) for fumes/dust, and PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) regulations for safety gear.
Because these are statutory, they are mandatory. You cannot 'opt out' of the EAWR.
misconceptionsToWatch: Only employers have legal duties under HASAWA.
EAWR is just a guide.
taskConstraints: Must mention HASAWA Section 2 and Section 7.
Must mention that EAWR contains 33 regulations.
steps.questionIntent: Identify specific statutory acts and their sections.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the hierarchy of statutory regulations.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which section of HASAWA specifically outlines the duties of the employee?
How many regulations are contained within the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR)?
What is the common term for HASAWA because it covers all other specific safety laws?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the relationship between the 'Umbrella' HASAWA and the specific EAWR ensure that electrical risks are managed legally?
deeperAnswerGuidance: HASAWA provides the general safety framework; EAWR provides the specific legal requirements for electricity; Both are statutory and mandatory; EAWR targets specific hazards under the general HASAWA duty
steps.hint: One sets the general rule for all jobs, the other sets the specific rules for wires and power.

steps.title: Non-statutory Regulations and Guidance
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
anchorFacts: BS 7671 (The IET Wiring Regulations), often called 'The Big Blue Book', is the national standard for electrical installations in the UK.
BS 7671 is the primary method used to demonstrate compliance with Part P of the Building Regulations in domestic settings.
The On-Site Guide (OSG) is a non-statutory 'pocket book' that simplifies BS 7671 for common, everyday installation tasks.
Guidance Notes (GN) provide even deeper technical detail on specific topics like testing or earthing. These documents tell you 'how' to install to the standard required by the law (EAWR).
misconceptionsToWatch: BS 7671 is a statutory law.
The On-Site Guide can replace the full Wiring Regulations in all scenarios.
taskConstraints: Must link BS 7671 to Part P of the Building Regulations.
Must use the term 'National Standard'.
steps.questionIntent: Identify key non-statutory documents and their purpose.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the technical necessity of guidance.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the alphanumeric code for the IET Wiring Regulations?
Which part of the Building Regulations does BS 7671 help electricians comply with in homes?
What is BS 7671 considered to be for electrical installations in the UK?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If the EAWR (Statutory) says an installation must be safe, why does an electrician need BS 7671 (Non-statutory) to actually do the work?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The law states the requirement but not the method; BS 7671 provides specific technical instructions like cable sizing; It defines the industry benchmark for safety; The law is too general for technical design
steps.hint: The law says 'be safe', but does the law tell you what size fuse to use?

steps.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Acts
steps.objective: Identify and map common statutory regulations used in the industry.
anchorFacts: PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) ensures that tools, ladders, and machinery are maintained and safe for use.
CDM (Construction Design and Management) Regulations manage safety during building projects, including the creation of the 'Health and Safety File'.
ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations) mainly concerns the power suppliers (DNOs) and their equipment, such as the service cut-out and meter.
Manual Handling Operations Regulations cover the safe way to lift and move heavy items like cable drums or motors.
All of these are Statutory, meaning they are legally binding and enforceable by the HSE.
misconceptionsToWatch: PUWER only applies to heavy machinery, not hand tools.
ESQCR is the same as EAWR.
taskConstraints: Must provide practical examples for PUWER (tools/ladders) and ESQCR (supplier equipment).
steps.questionIntent: Identify specific statutory regulations by their acronyms and scope.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the overlap of statutory duties.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation covers the safety and maintenance of power tools and ladders?
answerGuidance: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: Which regulations specifically concern the equipment owned by the electricity supplier, such as the meter?
answerGuidance: Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: Which regulations require the management of safety and the 'Health and Safety File' on construction sites?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason an electrician be aware of PUWER even if they are already following the electrical-specific EAWR?
deeperAnswerGuidance: EAWR covers the system safety; PUWER covers the tool safety; You can install a safe system using an unsafe ladder; Both are separate legal requirements
steps.hint: Think about the difference between the wires you are installing and the drill you are using to install them.

steps.title: Worked Example: Classifying Site Documents
steps.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are setting up a site office for a new industrial project. You have a copy of the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), the IET On-Site Guide, and the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA).
Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for 'Acts' or 'Regulations' that are mandatory laws. HASAWA is the Umbrella Act (Statutory). EAWR is the specific electrical law (Statutory).
Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for technical handbooks. The On-Site Guide is a simplified version of BS 7671 (Non-statutory).
Step 3: Determine the legal weight. If you follow the On-Site Guide, you are using a 'deemed to satisfy' method to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.
Final Classification: HASAWA and EAWR = Statutory. On-Site Guide = Non-statutory.
taskConstraints: Must use a scenario involving at least three specific documents.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are setting up a site office for a new industrial project. You have a copy of the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), the IET On-Site Guide, and the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA).
steps: Identify the Statutory Regulations (Mandatory Laws).
Identify the Non-statutory Guidance (Technical Methods).
Determine how they interact (Guidance helps meet the Law).
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Statutory regulations are the mandatory 'what' (the law), while non-statutory guidance is the technical 'how' (the method).

steps.hint: Always ask: 'Is this a law passed by Parliament, or a technical manual written by engineers?'

steps.title: Guided Practice: Categorising Site Safety and Technical Documents
steps.objective: To correctly identify statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance in a site-based scenario.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are on a construction site. You are wearing a hard hat (PPE Regulations), you are checking the safe lifting weight of a motor (Manual Handling Regulations), and you are looking up the correct mounting height for a socket in BS 7671.
Task: Categorize these three items into Statutory and Non-statutory.
taskConstraints: Ensure the learner identifies both the document and its legal status.
steps.questionIntent: To correctly identify statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance in a site-based scenario.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are on a construction site. You are wearing a hard hat (PPE Regulations), you are checking the safe lifting weight of a motor (Manual Handling Regulations), and you are looking up the correct mounting height for a socket in BS 7671.
steps: Identify the two items that are mandatory laws (Statutory).
Identify the one item that is a technical standard (Non-statutory).
requiredOutputs: Statutory: PPE Regulations and Manual Handling Regulations. Non-statutory: BS 7671.

steps.questionText: Based on the scenario, which items are Statutory Regulations and which is Non-statutory Guidance?
answerGuidance: PPE Regulations and Manual Handling Regulations. Non-statutory: BS 7671.
steps.hint: Regulations and Acts are usually the law. BS numbers refer to technical standards.

steps.title: Practice: Distinguishing Legal Status
steps.objective: To independently classify industry documents and explain their legal distinction.
anchorFacts: An apprentice asks why they need to carry both the EAWR and the On-Site Guide.
The EAWR is the law (Statutory) and the On-Site Guide is the technical instruction (Non-statutory).
taskConstraints: The learner must provide the correct classification for both documents.
steps.questionIntent: To independently classify industry documents and explain their legal distinction.

taskSkeleton.scenario: An apprentice asks why they need to carry both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the On-Site Guide (OSG).
steps: Explain the legal status of each document and why both are necessary.
requiredOutputs: EAWR is Statutory and the On-Site Guide is Non-statutory. The EAWR is a mandatory law, while the OSG provides the technical methods to comply with that law.

steps.questionText: Explain the legal status of the EAWR and the On-Site Guide, and why an electrician needs both.
answerGuidance: EAWR is Statutory and the On-Site Guide is Non-statutory. The EAWR is a mandatory law, while the OSG provides the technical methods to comply with that law.
steps.hint: One is the 'What' (the law) and the other is the 'How' (the technical guide).

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: Laws passed by Parliament that are mandatory and must be followed; failure to comply is a criminal offense.
terms.misconception: Thinking these are optional guidelines rather than legal requirements that can lead to prosecution.

terms.simpleDefinition: Industry-standard guidance and codes of practice that describe how to work safely to meet legal requirements.
terms.misconception: Believing they can be ignored because they aren't 'laws'; they are often used in court to prove a worker was competent.

terms.term: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
terms.simpleDefinition: The primary UK law that sets out the general duties of employers and employees to ensure safety in the workplace.
terms.misconception: Thinking it only places responsibility on the employer; employees also have legal duties under this Act.

terms.simpleDefinition: Statutory regulations that specifically focus on preventing death or injury from electrical causes during work activities.
terms.misconception: Confusing these legal requirements with the technical rules found in BS 7671.

terms.simpleDefinition: The UK national standard for electrical installations, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations.
terms.misconception: Believing BS 7671 is a statutory law; it is actually non-statutory guidance, though it is the industry benchmark.

terms.simpleDefinition: Equipment worn by a worker to minimize exposure to specific hazards, such as hard hats, boots, or gloves.
terms.misconception: Thinking PPE is the first way to solve a safety problem; it should only be used when the hazard cannot be removed.

terms.simpleDefinition: Statutory regulations requiring employers to control substances that can harm health, such as fumes, dust, or chemicals.
terms.misconception: Thinking it only applies to liquid chemicals; it also covers things like wood dust or solder fumes.

terms.simpleDefinition: A non-statutory handbook that provides a simplified version of BS 7671 for common electrical installation work.
terms.misconception: Thinking the On-Site Guide replaces the full BS 7671 for complex or industrial installations.

anchorPhrases: Statutory means it is the law.
EAWR is the law; BS 7671 is the method.
HASAWA covers everyone in every job.
misconceptionTargets: Any related term means the same thing in every context.
Non-statutory guidance has no legal weight in court.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO1 - Implications of electrical industry regulations

Lesson: 203-1A - Statutory and Non-statutory Regulations

Topic focus: Statutory and Non-statutory Regulations

Must-have topics: statutory regulations; non-statutory regulations/guidance

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO1.AC1.1: Identify statutory regulations
- 203.LO1.AC1.2: Identify non-statutory regulations/guidance

Lesson split rationale: Groups the identification of the two main categories of regulations and guidance used in the electrical industry.

In-scope teaching anchors: statutory regulations; non-statutory regulations/guidance

Out-of-scope topics: Implications of Regulatory Non-compliance

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Critical rules:
- One explanation object per teach/check section.
- Each explanation should be roughly 90-150 words when joined.
- Teach before test: every later question must be answerable from this prose.
- Use the vocabulary output naturally, not as a glossary dump.
- If a later check depends on an acronym or code letter, state that mapping explicitly in the explanation text.
- If a later check depends on a performance claim or advantage, explain the physical or practical reason in plain language.
- Do not overpack several concepts into one teach/check explanation just because the later lesson has practice stages.

teachChecks.title: The Purpose of Industry Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Explain why the electrical industry is governed by specific regulations and guidance.
retrievalTextLines: In the electrical industry, regulations exist to ensure safety, consistency, and legal accountability. Electricity is inherently dangerous; it can cause fires, serious injury, or death if not handled correctly. Because of this, we cannot leave installation methods to personal preference.

Regulations provide a common set of rules so that every electrician in the UK works to the same high standard. This consistency means that any qualified person can walk onto a site and understand how the system was built. These rules are not just 'paperwork' or 'red tape'; they are the minimum requirements necessary to protect the public and the workforce. By following these standards, we ensure that installations are reliable and, most importantly, safe for continued use.
teachChecks.hint: Think about why we need everyone to follow the same safety rules instead of making them up as they go.

teachChecks.title: The Two Categories of Rules
teachChecks.objective: Differentiate between Statutory and Non-statutory regulations.
retrievalTextLines: All rules in our industry fall into two main categories: Statutory and Non-statutory. It is vital to understand the difference between a 'law' and 'guidance'.

Statutory means it is the law of the land, passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to fines or imprisonment. Non-statutory regulations are expert guidance and codes of practice. These are the 'how-to' manuals written by industry bodies. While a non-statutory document itself is not a law, it is often used in court to prove whether or not a person was working competently. If you follow the non-statutory guidance, you are usually meeting your statutory legal duties.
teachChecks.hint: One is a law passed by Parliament, the other is expert guidance on how to do the job.

teachChecks.title: Statutory Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
retrievalTextLines: Statutory regulations are mandatory requirements that must be followed. The primary law is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This acts as an 'Umbrella Act,' setting out general safety duties for everyone at work.

Underneath this umbrella, we have more specific laws. The Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is the most important statutory document for us, as it focuses specifically on electrical hazards. Other examples include COSHH, which stands for Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (covering things like solder fumes or dust), and PPE, which stands for Personal Protective Equipment. PPE regulations require you to use safety gear like hard hats and boots. Because these are statutory, failure to comply is a legal breach, not just a technical mistake.
teachChecks.hint: Statutory regulations like EAWR and HASAWA are mandatory laws.

teachChecks.title: Non-statutory Regulations and Guidance
teachChecks.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
retrievalTextLines: Non-statutory regulations are the professional's guidebooks. The most famous example is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' Although BS 7671 is not a law passed by Parliament, it is the national standard that tells us exactly how to design and install electrical systems safely.

Other non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified 'Pocket Guide' for common tasks, and various Guidance Notes. These documents are essential because they provide the technical detail that the law (like EAWR) does not. For example, the law says an installation must be safe; BS 7671 tells you exactly what size cable to use to make it safe. Following these ensures you are working to the current industry benchmark.
teachChecks.hint: BS 7671 is the main industry standard, but it is technically non-statutory guidance.

teachChecks.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Acts
teachChecks.objective: Identify and map common statutory regulations used in the industry.
retrievalTextLines: To work legally, you must recognize the specific names of statutory regulations. HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) is the foundation for all workplace safety. EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) specifically targets electrical safety in the workplace.

Other key laws include PUWER, which stands for Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations; this ensures your tools and machinery are safe to use. ESQCR stands for Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, which mainly concerns the companies that supply and distribute power. CDM stands for Construction (Design and Management) Regulations, which manage safety during building projects. Each of these is a statutory requirement, meaning they are legally binding. You must use these specific titles and acronyms correctly when identifying the laws that govern your work.
teachChecks.hint: Look for acronyms like HASAWA, EAWR, and PUWER; these are all statutory laws.

teachChecks.title: The Purpose of Industry Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary reason for having industry regulations in electrical work?
What inherent risk of electricity makes these regulations necessary?
What does consistency allow a qualified person to do when walking onto a new site?
answerGuidance: Understand how the system was built

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does having a common set of rules across the UK improve safety for an electrician taking over a job from someone else?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures the previous worker used the same standards
Removes guesswork regarding installation methods
Allows any qualified person to understand the system layout
Prevents dangerous variations based on personal preference
teachChecks.hint: Think about why we need everyone to follow the same safety rules instead of making them up as they go.

teachChecks.title: The Two Categories of Rules

basicQuestions.questionText: What term describes a rule that is the law of the land passed by Parliament?
What type of offense is committed if a statutory regulation is broken?
What category do expert guidance and codes of practice fall into?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is it important for an electrician to follow non-statutory guidance even if it is not a law passed by Parliament?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Used in court to prove competence
Demonstrates the worker met statutory legal duties
Provides the technical method to comply with the law
Acts as the industry benchmark for safety
teachChecks.hint: One is a law passed by Parliament, the other is expert guidance on how to do the job.

teachChecks.title: Statutory Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the full name of the 'Umbrella Act' for workplace safety?
answerGuidance: Health and Safety at Work Act

basicQuestions.questionText: Which specific statutory regulation focuses on electrical hazards in the workplace?
What does the acronym COSHH stand for?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the relationship between HASAWA and EAWR ensure that electrical risks are managed legally?
deeperAnswerGuidance: HASAWA provides the general safety framework
EAWR provides the specific legal requirements for electricity
Both are statutory and mandatory to follow
EAWR sits under the HASAWA umbrella to target specific hazards
teachChecks.hint: Statutory regulations like EAWR and HASAWA are mandatory laws.

teachChecks.title: Non-statutory Regulations and Guidance

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the common name for the BS 7671 national standard?
Which non-statutory document acts as a simplified pocket guide for common tasks?
What do non-statutory documents provide that the law usually lacks?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: If the law (EAWR) says an installation must be safe, why is BS 7671 necessary for a working electrician?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The law states the requirement but not the method
BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions
It defines exact requirements like cable sizes
It ensures the installation meets the industry benchmark for safety
teachChecks.hint: BS 7671 is the main industry standard, but it is technically non-statutory guidance.

teachChecks.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Acts

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation ensures that tools and machinery are safe to use?
answerGuidance: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the acronym ESQCR stand for?
answerGuidance: Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: Which regulations manage safety specifically during building and construction projects?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why must an electrician be aware of PUWER in addition to EAWR when performing a task?
deeperAnswerGuidance: EAWR covers the electrical safety of the system
PUWER covers the safety of the tools being used
Both are separate statutory requirements that must be met
Ensures both the work activity and the equipment are legal
teachChecks.hint: Look for acronyms like HASAWA, EAWR, and PUWER; these are all statutory laws.

You need to categorize these correctly before starting the Risk Assessment.",

parsed.title: Worked Example: Classifying Site Documents
parsed.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are preparing to install a new sub-main in a commercial unit. On your desk, you have three documents: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). You need to categorize these correctly before starting the Risk Assessment.

Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for the 'Acts' or 'Regulations' passed by Parliament. HASAWA is the 'Umbrella Act' and is statutory. The EAWR specifically covers electrical safety and is also statutory. These are the laws of the land; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.

Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for the technical standards and codes of practice. BS 7671 (The Big Blue Book) is the national standard for electrical installations. While it is the industry benchmark, it is non-statutory guidance. It tells you 'how' to install the cable to meet the 'legal' requirements set out in the EAWR.
Step 3: Apply the priority. You recognize that while you use BS 7671 to design the circuit, you are doing so to ensure you do not breach the statutory requirements of the EAWR.
Final Result: HASAWA and EAWR are classified as Statutory Regulations (The Law). BS 7671 is classified as Non-statutory Guidance (The Method).

Takeaway: Statutory regulations are mandatory laws you must obey to avoid prosecution; non-statutory guidance provides the technical instructions to ensure your work is safe enough to meet those laws.
parsed.hint: Always ask: 'Is this a law passed by Parliament, or is it a technical manual on how to do the job?'

{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "Statutory Regulations",
      "simpleDefinition": "Laws passed by Parliament that are mandatory and must be followed; failure to comply is a criminal offense.",
      "anchor": "The Law of the Land",
      "misconception": "Thinking these are optional guidelines rather than legal requirements that can lead to prosecution."
    },
    {
      "term": "Non-statutory Regulations",
      "simpleDefinition": "Industry-standard guidance and codes of practice that describe how to work safely to meet legal requirements.",
      "anchor": "The Professional's Guidebook",
      "misconception": "Believing they can be ignored because they aren't 'laws'; they are often used in court to prove a worker was competent."
    },
    {
      "term": "HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)",
      "simpleDefinition": "The primary UK law that sets out the general duties of employers and employees to ensure safety in the workplace.",
      "anchor": "The Umbrella Act",
      "misconception": "Thinking it only places responsibility on the employer; employees also have legal duties under this Act."
    },
    {
      "term": "EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations)",
      "simpleDefinition": "Statutory regulations that specifically focus on preventing death or injury from electrical causes during work activities.",
      "anchor": "The Electrician's Law",
      "misconception": "Confusing these legal requirements with the technical rules found in BS 7671."
    },
    {
      "term": "BS 7671",
      "simpleDefinition": "The UK national standard for electrical installations, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations.",
      "anchor": "The Big Blue Book",
      "misconception": "Believing BS 7671 is a statutory law; it is actually non-statutory guidance, though it is the industry benchmark."
    },
    {
      "term": "PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)",
      "simpleDefinition": "Equipment worn by a worker to minimize exposure to specific hazards, such as hard hats, boots, or gloves.",
      "anchor": "The Last Line of Defence",
      "misconception": "Thinking PPE is the first way to solve a safety problem; it should only be used when the hazard cannot be removed."
    },
    {
      "term": "COSHH",
      "simpleDefinition": "Statutory regulations requiring employers to control substances that can harm health, such as fumes, dust, or chemicals.",
      "anchor": "Chemicals and Dust",
      "misconception": "Thinking it only applies to liquid chemicals; it also covers things like wood dust or solder fumes."
    },
    {
      "term": "On-Site Guide",
      "simpleDefinition": "A non-statutory handbook that provides a simplified version of BS 7671 for common electrical installation work.",
      "anchor": "The Pocket Guide",
      "misconception": "Thinking the On-Site Guide replaces the full BS 7671 for complex or industrial installations."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "Statutory means it is the law.",
    "Non-statutory is the expert guidance.",
    "EAWR is the law; BS 7671 is the method.",
    "HASAWA covers everyone in every job."
  ],

    "Any related term means the same thing in every context.",
    "Non-statutory guidance has no legal weight in court.",

guidedPractice.title: Guided Practice: Categorising Site Safety and Technical Documents
guidedPractice.objective: To correctly identify statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance in a site-based scenario.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are working on a new build. You are wearing safety boots (PPE Regulations), you have just read the safety data sheet for a new adhesive (COSHH), and you are checking a cable size in your On-Site Guide.
Step 1: Look for the 'Statutory' items. These are the laws of the land. PPE Regulations and COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) are both legal requirements passed by Parliament.
Step 2: Look for the 'Non-statutory' items. These are the technical guides. The On-Site Guide is a handbook that provides guidance on how to meet the standards, but it is not a law itself.
Step 3: Group them clearly into the two categories.
guidedPractice.questionText: Based on the scenario, identify which items are Statutory Regulations and which item is Non-statutory Guidance.
answerGuidance: Statutory Regulations: PPE Regulations and COSHH. Non-statutory Guidance: On-Site Guide.
guidedPractice.hint: Remember: Regulations and Acts are usually statutory (the law), while guides and handbooks are non-statutory.

practice.title: Practice: Distinguishing Legal Status
practice.objective: To independently classify industry documents and explain their legal distinction.
retrievalTextLines: A trainee asks you why they need to look at both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671).
You need to explain the status of each document to help them understand which one is the law and which one is the technical method used to comply with that law.
practice.questionText: Classify the EAWR and BS 7671 as either statutory or non-statutory, and state the primary legal difference between these two categories.
answerGuidance: EAWR is Statutory (the law/mandatory) and BS 7671 is Non-statutory (technical guidance/the method). The primary difference is that statutory regulations are mandatory laws where failure to comply is a criminal offence, whereas non-statutory guidance provides the professional standards to meet those laws.
practice.hint: Think about which one is 'The Electrician's Law' and which one is 'The Big Blue Book' of technical rules.

This consistency means that any qualified person can walk onto a site and understand how the system was built. Non-statutory regulations are expert guidance and codes of practice. These are the 'how-to' manuals written by industry bodies. While a non-statutory document itself is not a law, it is often used in court to prove whether or not a person was working competently. The Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is the most important statutory document for us, as it focuses specifically on electrical hazards. - Make the question synthesize the lesson ideas.
- Use one new applied scenario, not the exact worked-example scenario repeated.
- Prefer one integrative task with one coherent response target, not a loose list of mini-questions.
- Do not combine factual recall, list-making, and open explanation in the same response field.

- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in the integrative phase. Ask for one applied explanation that naturally includes the required relationship.

integrative.title: Integrative Close: The Relationship Between Law and Guidance
integrative.objective: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a professional electrical context.
retrievalTextLines: In the electrical industry, we don't just pick one set of rules to follow. We use statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance together to ensure our work is both legal and technically sound.

Statutory regulations like the EAWR set the mandatory legal requirements, while non-statutory documents like BS 7671 provide the technical methods we use to meet those legal obligations. Understanding how these two categories interact is the mark of a professional electrician.
integrative.questionText: Imagine you are explaining to a new apprentice why they need to use both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671). Explain the different legal status of these two documents and how they work together to ensure a safe, legal installation.
answerGuidance: The response should identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation (the law/mandatory) and BS 7671 is non-statutory guidance (the technical standard). It must explain that they work together because the EAWR sets the legal safety requirements, while BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions and methods needed to achieve that legal level of safety.
integrative.hint: Think about which one is the 'Law of the Land' and which one is the 'Professional's Guidebook' that tells you how to meet that law.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To enable learners to correctly identify and distinguish between statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance within the UK electrical industry.
anchorFacts: To enable learners to correctly identify and distinguish between statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance within the UK electrical industry.
Why this matters: Understanding the 'why' prevents learners from viewing regulations as arbitrary paperwork.
taskConstraints: Introduce the lesson naturally, but do not start the first teaching chunk yet.

steps: The Purpose of Industry Regulations
Explanation B
Statutory Regulations
Non-statutory Regulations/guidance

steps.title: The Purpose of Industry Regulations
steps.objective: Explain why the electrical industry is governed by specific regulations and guidance.
anchorFacts: In the electrical industry, regulations exist to ensure safety, consistency, and legal accountability. Electricity is inherently dangerous; it can cause fires, serious injury, or death if not handled correctly. Because of this, we cannot leave installation methods to personal preference.

Regulations provide a common set of rules so that every electrician in the UK works to the same high standard. This consistency means that any qualified person can walk onto a site and understand how the system was built. These rules are not just 'paperwork' or 'red tape'; they are the minimum requirements necessary to protect the public and the workforce. By following these standards, we ensure that installations are reliable and, most importantly, safe for continued use.
misconceptionsToWatch: Regulations are only for large industrial sites.
Think about why we need everyone to follow the same safety rules instead of making them up as they go.
taskConstraints: The role of standards in ensuring safety, consistency, and legal compliance in electrical work.
Understanding the 'why' prevents learners from viewing regulations as arbitrary paperwork.
steps.questionIntent: The role of standards in ensuring safety, consistency, and legal compliance in electrical work.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary reason for having industry regulations in electrical work?
What inherent risk of electricity makes these regulations necessary?
What does consistency allow a qualified person to do when walking onto a new site?
answerGuidance: Understand how the system was built

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way having a common set of rules across the UK improve safety for an electrician taking over a job from someone else?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Understanding the 'why' prevents learners from viewing regulations as arbitrary paperwork.
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures the previous worker used the same standards; Removes guesswork regarding installation methods; Allows any qualified person to understand the system layout; Prevents dangerous variations based on personal preference
steps.hint: Think about why we need everyone to follow the same safety rules instead of making them up as they go.

steps.title: The Two Categories of Rules
steps.objective: Differentiate between Statutory and Non-statutory regulations.
anchorFacts: All rules in our industry fall into two main categories: Statutory and Non-statutory. It is vital to understand the difference between a 'law' and 'guidance'.

Statutory means it is the law of the land, passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to fines or imprisonment. Non-statutory regulations are expert guidance and codes of practice. These are the 'how-to' manuals written by industry bodies. While a non-statutory document itself is not a law, it is often used in court to prove whether or not a person was working competently. If you follow the non-statutory guidance, you are usually meeting your statutory legal duties.
misconceptionsToWatch: One is a law passed by Parliament, the other is expert guidance on how to do the job.
steps.questionIntent: Explanation B

basicQuestions.questionText: What term describes a rule that is the law of the land passed by Parliament?
What type of offense is committed if a statutory regulation is broken?
What category do expert guidance and codes of practice fall into?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it important for an electrician to follow non-statutory guidance even if it is not a law passed by Parliament?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explanation B
deeperAnswerGuidance: Used in court to prove competence; Demonstrates the worker met statutory legal duties; Provides the technical method to comply with the law; Acts as the industry benchmark for safety
steps.hint: One is a law passed by Parliament, the other is expert guidance on how to do the job.

steps.title: Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
anchorFacts: Statutory regulations are mandatory requirements that must be followed. The primary law is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This acts as an 'Umbrella Act,' setting out general safety duties for everyone at work.

Underneath this umbrella, we have more specific laws. The Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is the most important statutory document for us, as it focuses specifically on electrical hazards. Other examples include COSHH, which stands for Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (covering things like solder fumes or dust), and PPE, which stands for Personal Protective Equipment. PPE regulations require you to use safety gear like hard hats and boots. Because these are statutory, failure to comply is a legal breach, not just a technical mistake.
misconceptionsToWatch: Statutory regulations like EAWR and HASAWA are mandatory laws.
steps.questionIntent: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the full name of the 'Umbrella Act' for workplace safety?
answerGuidance: Health and Safety at Work Act

basicQuestions.questionText: Which specific statutory regulation focuses on electrical hazards in the workplace?
What does the acronym COSHH stand for?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the relationship between HASAWA and EAWR ensure that electrical risks are managed legally?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
deeperAnswerGuidance: HASAWA provides the general safety framework; EAWR provides the specific legal requirements for electricity; Both are statutory and mandatory to follow; EAWR sits under the HASAWA umbrella to target specific hazards
steps.hint: Statutory regulations like EAWR and HASAWA are mandatory laws.

steps.title: Non-statutory Regulations and Guidance
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
anchorFacts: Non-statutory regulations are the professional's guidebooks. The most famous example is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' Although BS 7671 is not a law passed by Parliament, it is the national standard that tells us exactly how to design and install electrical systems safely.

Other non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified 'Pocket Guide' for common tasks, and various Guidance Notes. These documents are essential because they provide the technical detail that the law (like EAWR) does not. For example, the law says an installation must be safe; BS 7671 tells you exactly what size cable to use to make it safe. Following these ensures you are working to the current industry benchmark.
misconceptionsToWatch: BS 7671 is the main industry standard, but it is technically non-statutory guidance.
steps.questionIntent: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the common name for the BS 7671 national standard?
Which non-statutory document acts as a simplified pocket guide for common tasks?
What do non-statutory documents provide that the law usually lacks?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If the law (EAWR) says an installation must be safe, why is BS 7671 necessary for a working electrician?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
deeperAnswerGuidance: The law states the requirement but not the method
BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions
It defines exact requirements like cable sizes
It ensures the installation meets the industry benchmark for safety
steps.hint: BS 7671 is the main industry standard, but it is technically non-statutory guidance.

steps.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Acts
steps.objective: Identify and map common statutory regulations used in the industry.
anchorFacts: To work legally, you must recognize the specific names of statutory regulations. HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) is the foundation for all workplace safety. EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) specifically targets electrical safety in the workplace.

Other key laws include PUWER, which stands for Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations; this ensures your tools and machinery are safe to use. ESQCR stands for Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, which mainly concerns the companies that supply and distribute power. CDM stands for Construction (Design and Management) Regulations, which manage safety during building projects. Each of these is a statutory requirement, meaning they are legally binding. You must use these specific titles and acronyms correctly when identifying the laws that govern your work.
misconceptionsToWatch: Look for acronyms like HASAWA, EAWR, and PUWER; these are all statutory laws.
steps.questionIntent: Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation ensures that tools and machinery are safe to use?
answerGuidance: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the acronym ESQCR stand for?
answerGuidance: Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: Which regulations manage safety specifically during building and construction projects?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason an electrician be aware of PUWER in addition to EAWR when performing a task?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.
deeperAnswerGuidance: EAWR covers the electrical safety of the system; PUWER covers the safety of the tools being used; Both are separate statutory requirements that must be met; Ensures both the work activity and the equipment are legal
steps.hint: Look for acronyms like HASAWA, EAWR, and PUWER; these are all statutory laws.

steps.title: Worked Example: Classifying Site Documents
steps.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are preparing to install a new sub-main in a commercial unit. On your desk, you have three documents: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). You need to categorize these correctly before starting the Risk Assessment.

Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for the 'Acts' or 'Regulations' passed by Parliament. HASAWA is the 'Umbrella Act' and is statutory. The EAWR specifically covers electrical safety and is also statutory. These are the laws of the land; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.

Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for the technical standards and codes of practice. BS 7671 (The Big Blue Book) is the national standard for electrical installations. While it is the industry benchmark, it is non-statutory guidance. It tells you 'how' to install the cable to meet the 'legal' requirements set out in the EAWR.
Step 3: Apply the priority. You recognize that while you use BS 7671 to design the circuit, you are doing so to ensure you do not breach the statutory requirements of the EAWR.
Final Result: HASAWA and EAWR are classified as Statutory Regulations (The Law). BS 7671 is classified as Non-statutory Guidance (The Method).

You need to categorize these correctly before starting the Risk Assessment.
steps: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for the 'Acts' or 'Regulations' passed by Parliament. HASAWA is the 'Umbrella Act' and is statutory. The EAWR specifically covers electrical safety and is also statutory. These are the laws of the land; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.

Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for the technical standards and codes of practice. BS 7671 (The Big Blue Book) is the national standard for electrical installations. While it is the industry benchmark, it is non-statutory guidance. It tells you 'how' to install the cable to meet the 'legal' requirements set out in the EAWR.
Apply the priority. You recognize that while you use BS 7671 to design the circuit, you are doing so to ensure you do not breach the statutory requirements of the EAWR.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Statutory regulations are mandatory laws you must obey to avoid prosecution; non-statutory guidance provides the technical instructions to ensure your work is safe enough to meet those laws.

steps.hint: Always ask: 'Is this a law passed by Parliament, or is it a technical manual on how to do the job?'

steps.title: Guided Practice: Categorising Site Safety and Technical Documents
steps.objective: To correctly identify statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance in a site-based scenario.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are working on a new build. You are wearing safety boots (PPE Regulations), you have just read the safety data sheet for a new adhesive (COSHH), and you are checking a cable size in your On-Site Guide.
Step 1: Look for the 'Statutory' items. These are the laws of the land. PPE Regulations and COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) are both legal requirements passed by Parliament.
Step 2: Look for the 'Non-statutory' items. These are the technical guides. The On-Site Guide is a handbook that provides guidance on how to meet the standards, but it is not a law itself.
Step 3: Group them clearly into the two categories.
steps.questionIntent: To correctly identify statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance in a site-based scenario.

taskSkeleton.scenario: Scenario: You are working on a new build. You are wearing safety boots (PPE Regulations), you have just read the safety data sheet for a new adhesive (COSHH), and you are checking a cable size in your On-Site Guide.

Step 2: Look for the 'Non-statutory' items. These are the technical guides. The On-Site Guide is a handbook that provides guidance on how to meet the standards, but it is not a law itself.
Step 3: Group them clearly into the two categories.
requiredOutputs: PPE Regulations and COSHH. Non-statutory Guidance: On-Site Guide.

steps.questionText: Based on the scenario, identify which items are Statutory Regulations and which item is Non-statutory Guidance.
answerGuidance: PPE Regulations and COSHH. Non-statutory Guidance: On-Site Guide.
steps.hint: Remember: Regulations and Acts are usually statutory (the law), while guides and handbooks are non-statutory.

steps.title: Practice: Distinguishing Legal Status
steps.objective: To independently classify industry documents and explain their legal distinction.
anchorFacts: A trainee asks you why they need to look at both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671).
You need to explain the status of each document to help them understand which one is the law and which one is the technical method used to comply with that law.
steps.questionIntent: To independently classify industry documents and explain their legal distinction.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A trainee asks you why they need to look at both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671).
steps: You need to explain the status of each document to help them understand which one is the law and which one is the technical method used to comply with that law.
requiredOutputs: EAWR is Statutory and BS 7671 is Non-statutory. The primary difference is that statutory regulations are mandatory laws where failure to comply is a criminal offence, whereas non-statutory guidance provides the professional standards to meet those laws.

steps.questionText: From the scenario, identify the specific technical answer that best fits.
answerGuidance: EAWR is Statutory and BS 7671 is Non-statutory. The primary difference is that statutory regulations are mandatory laws where failure to comply is a criminal offence, whereas non-statutory guidance provides the professional standards to meet those laws.
steps.hint: Think about which one is 'The Electrician's Law' and which one is 'The Big Blue Book' of technical rules.

steps.title: Integrative Close: The Relationship Between Law and Guidance
steps.objective: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a professional electrical context.
anchorFacts: In the electrical industry, we don't just pick one set of rules to follow. We use statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance together to ensure our work is both legal and technically sound.

Statutory regulations like the EAWR set the mandatory legal requirements, while non-statutory documents like BS 7671 provide the technical methods we use to meet those legal obligations. Understanding how these two categories interact is the mark of a professional electrician.
steps.questionIntent: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a professional electrical context.

taskSkeleton.scenario: In the electrical industry, we don't just pick one set of rules to follow. We use statutory regulations and non-statutory guidance together to ensure our work is both legal and technically sound.
steps: Statutory regulations like the EAWR set the mandatory legal requirements, while non-statutory documents like BS 7671 provide the technical methods we use to meet those legal obligations. Understanding how these two categories interact is the mark of a professional electrician.
requiredOutputs: The response should identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation and BS 7671 is non-statutory guidance . It must explain that they work together because the EAWR sets the legal safety requirements, while BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions and methods needed to achieve that legal level of safety.

steps.questionText: Imagine you are explaining to a new apprentice why they need to use both the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671). Explain the different legal status of these two documents and how they work together to ensure a safe, legal installation.
answerGuidance: The response should identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation and BS 7671 is non-statutory guidance. It must explain that they work together because the EAWR sets the legal safety requirements, while BS 7671 provides the specific technical instructions and methods needed to achieve that legal level of safety.
steps.hint: Think about which one is the 'Law of the Land' and which one is the 'Professional's Guidebook' that tells you how to meet that law.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review
spacedReview.objective: Recall the shared responsibility for health and safety in the workplace as established in basic safety legislation.
retrievalTextLines: Think back to your initial health and safety induction.
The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA) sets the ground rules for every workplace in the UK.
It establishes that safety isn't just one person's job; it is a collective duty.
spacedReview.questionText: Under the Health and Safety at Work Act, who is legally responsible for ensuring safety on a worksite: the employer, the employee, or both?
answerGuidance: Employers have a 'duty of care' to provide a safe system of work, proper tools, and necessary training.
Employees have a legal duty to follow those safety systems and take reasonable care of themselves and others affected by their work.

        "Statutory means it is the law of the land, passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to fines or imprisonment. Non-statutory regulations are expert guidance and codes of practice. These are the 'how-to' manuals written by industry bodies. While a non-statutory document itself is not a law, it is often used in court to prove whether or not a person was working competently. If you follow the non-statutory guidance, you are usually meeting your statutory legal duties."
      ],
